metadata.title:
HAVE SOCIALISTS
RIGHT TO MEET


IN STREETS?

pages.raw_text:
SEATTLE, Wash, Oct. 1870
cialists all over the United States
are watching with interest mo-
mentous clash between socialists
OF this city and the authorities over
the right Of the former to use the
streets for free speech.


Wholesale arrests have been
made, much agitation has been
aroused and the socialists will
carry the legal question to the SUI
preme court.


A test case will settle the point
that has been raised at one time
or another in nearly every city in
the country.


Many prominent men have been
converted to socialism through the
strife, and Rev. w. D. Simonds,
pastor of the Boylston avenue Uni
tartan church, has taken the
stump in favor of the socialists.


Open warfare was brought about
when the police raided a meeting
several evenings ago, arresting
speakers. Each deposited 500 for
his appearance in court to answer
to the charge of constructing the
sidewalks by speaking or dirorderly
conduct"


The socialists take the position
